title: One Outlet Called the Canada Tariffs Illegal. The Other 27 Called Them 'Obscure.'
subtitle: The legal status of Section 338 of the 1930 Smoot-Hawley Act is not a settled question — but almost no one said so
body_md:
Trump's 50% tariffs on Canadian goods, announced July 20 under Section 338 of the 1930 Smoot-Hawley Act, generated 28 outlets' worth of coverage that divided cleanly into two postures: treating the legal authority as settled, or treating it as contested. Only [Reason](https://reason.com/volokh/2026/07/20/trump-imposes-new-illegal-tariffs-on-canada/) took a position: "The 50% tariffs on many Canadian imports are based on the long-defunct Section 338 of the notorious Smoot-Hawley Tariff Act of 1930." The magazine cited Georgetown and Hoover Institution legal scholarship arguing the provision was superseded by legislation in 1962 and 1974 and has not been invoked since at least the 1940s. The other 27 outlets described Section 338 as "obscure" or "untested in court" — a characterisation that stops short of Reason's claim but also stops short of endorsing the administration's position.

[BBC](https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cg4dzq3x3e1o) captured the modal approach: noting that Trump's action "uses a different, obscure law that is untested in court" without adjudicating whether the legal basis is valid. [Politico Europe](https://www.politico.com/news/2026/07/20/trump-threatens-50-percent-tariff-on-canadian-goods-01005548) framed the tariffs as "upping the ante in North American trade." [Breitbart](https://www.breitbart.com/politics/2026/07/20/trump-imposes-50-percent-tariff-on-variety-of-canadian-goods-over-discrimination-of-american-autos/) reproduced the full White House proclamation language without legal analysis. The spectrum runs from Reason's "illegal" to Breitbart's implicit endorsement, with the majority of the press occupying a middle position of procedural description that avoids the underlying legal dispute.

The strategic reading that distinguished the Canadian perspective came from [CBC](https://www.cbc.ca/news/world/trump-tariffs-canada-why-now-9.7277446?cmp=rss), which was alone in articulating the tactical logic: the 30-day delay before the tariffs take effect is a deliberate pressure lever, designed to strip Canada's negotiating leverage in ongoing CUSMA renegotiation talks. [El Pais English](https://english.elpais.com/usa/2026-07-20/trump-imposes-new-50-tariffs-on-canada-amid-usmca-trade-talks.html) reconstructed the pre-announcement sequence — Trump raised wildfire smoke grievances and dairy discrimination claims in the days before the announcement, setting up the rationale — in detail no other outlet matched. [The Independent](https://www.independent.co.uk/news/world/americas/us-politics/trump-tariffs-canada-trade-carney-b3018660.html) was alone in quoting Canadian Prime Minister Carney calling the tariffs a "direct violation" of USMCA.

[Globe and Mail](https://www.theglobeandmail.com/world/us-politics/article-trump-tariffs-canada-news-july-20/) and [Daily Maverick](https://www.dailymaverick.co.za/article/2026-07-21-us-imposes-new-50-tariffs-on-20-billion-worth-of-canadian-products/) reported the scope: approximately $20 billion in Canadian goods affected, covering auto parts, dairy, wine, and furniture. The Canadian press treated this as a trade war escalation with a specific tactical purpose; the international press treated it as a diplomatic confrontation without engaging the CUSMA leverage argument that CBCs reporting surfaced.

Three silences mark the gaps. Mexico, the third USMCA partner whose trade relationship with the United States is directly affected by any renegotiation, produced no coverage. France, whose wine industry competes with Canadian wine now under US tariff pressure, was absent. Australia, a Commonwealth country closely aligned with Canada and exposed to US tariff-policy signals, did not appear. The diplomatic-economic web around the Canada tariffs is considerably wider than the bilateral framing the coverage applied.

What the corpus does not resolve is the legal question Reason raised. If Section 338 is defunct — superseded rather than merely unused — then these tariffs are not merely politically controversial but legally void, susceptible to court challenge, and potentially reversible on grounds that have nothing to do with negotiating outcomes. Whether the Canadian government pursues that argument in US courts, or whether US courts entertain it, is the dimension the other 27 outlets effectively decided not to examine.
